# Part 9 — The Lab {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## Meet the Breadboard

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="60%"}

A breadboard lets you build circuits **without soldering** — perfect for experimenting safely.

::: {.fragment}
- **Terminal rows** (short rows, A–E / F–J) — each row of 5 holes is **one wire**
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Centre divider** — separates left and right; designed to straddle chips
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="40%"}

![](assets/breadboard_diagram.png){width="100%"}

::: {.callout-note}
## The golden rule
Same row = same wire. Different row = different wire.
:::

::::

:::::

## Powering the Board: The Rails

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}

- **Power rails** (long side columns, + and −) — connect to **3.3V** and **GND**
- Usually marked with red (+) and blue (−) lines.
- Power flows the entire length of the rail.

::: {.callout-note .fragment}
## What is GND?
**GND (Ground)** = the − terminal = **0 V reference point**.
All voltages are measured *relative to GND* — it is the drain at the bottom of the water tank.
Every circuit needs a complete loop: current must be able to flow **from + to GND**.
:::

::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}

![](assets/breadboard_diagram.png){width="100%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro tip
Connect one rail to **5V** and the other to **GND** first — then you can pull power from anywhere on the board.
:::

::::

:::::

## Build #1 — Calculate Before You Wire

Before connecting anything, calculate the **safe resistor** for each LED.

::: {.fragment}

| | Value |
|---|---|
| Arduino supply voltage | **5.0 V** |
| LED forward voltage | **2.0 V** |
| Voltage across resistor | **5.0 − 2.0 = 3.0 V** |
| Target current | **10 mA = 0.01 A** |
| **Resistor needed** | **R = 3.0 / 0.01 = 300 Ω** |

:::

::: {.fragment}

::: {.callout-tip}

## Closest standard value: **330 Ω** ✓
Slightly higher = slightly less current = LED lives longer. Never go lower.

:::

:::

## Build #1 — Traffic Light on Wokwi {background-color="#f7f9fc"}

<iframe src="https://wokwi.com/projects/460363431768902657
        width="100%" height="520px"
        style="border:none; border-radius:12px;"
        allowfullscreen></iframe>

*Build this circuit in Wokwi. Run it. Does the sequence match your code?*

## Build #1 — Arduino Code

```cpp
// Define each LED pin
const int red    = 2;
const int yellow = 3;
const int green  = 4;

void setup() {
  pinMode(red, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(yellow, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(green, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(red, HIGH);    delay(5000);   // Red ON for 5 s
  digitalWrite(red, LOW);
  digitalWrite(yellow, HIGH); delay(2000);   // Yellow ON for 2 s
  digitalWrite(yellow, LOW);
  digitalWrite(green, HIGH);  delay(5000);   // Green ON for 5 s
  digitalWrite(green, LOW);
}
```


## Extend It with AI {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

Your traffic light works. Now **ask AI to extend it.**

> *"I have an Arduino sketch cycling Red (Pin 2) → Yellow (Pin 3) → Green (Pin 4).*
> *I want to add a button on Pin 12 that, when held, keeps the green light on for 10 s instead of 5 s.*
> *Show me the updated code and explain what changed."*



## Build #2 — The Voltage Divider

How does a **light sensor** become a number the Arduino can read?

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}

::: {.fragment}

```
  5V ── 10 kΩ ──●── LDR ── GND
                ↑
           ADC reads here
               (A0)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}

- LDR resistance **drops** in bright light
- Fixed 10 kΩ stays **constant**
- Midpoint voltage **rises** as LDR gets smaller

:::

::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.callout-tip}

## Water analogy
Two resistors in series = two narrow pipes.
The pressure tap *between* them reads a fraction of total — depending on which section is narrower.

:::

::: {.fragment}

| Light level | LDR R | Midpoint V |
|---|---|---|
| Bright | ~1 kΩ | ~0.5 V |
| Dark | ~100 kΩ | ~4.5 V |

:::

::::

:::::

## The ADC — Bridging Analog and Digital

The Arduino thinks in **digital** (0s and 1s). The LDR gives us **analog** (a continuous voltage).
The **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** is the translator.

::::: {.columns}

:::: {.column width="55%"}

::: {.fragment}

- The ADC samples the **midpoint voltage** thousands of times per second
- It maps that voltage to a **10-bit number**: 0 → 1023

:::

::: {.fragment}

| Voltage | `analogRead()` value |
|---------|----------------------|
| 0 V (max bright) | 0 |
| 2.5 V (half) | ~512 |
| 5.0 V (max dark) | 1023 |

:::

::::

:::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.callout-tip .fragment}

## Why 1023?
10 bits can hold 2¹⁰ = **1,024** values (0 to 1023).
Arduino Uno uses 10-bit resolution for its analog pins.

:::

::: {.callout-note .fragment}

## In code
`int val = analogRead(A0);` — take one sample from analog pin 0 and return the number.

:::

::::

:::::

## Build #2 — Automatic Light on Wokwi {background-color="#f7f9fc"}

<iframe src="https://wokwi.com/projects/427993202117173249/embed"
        width="100%" height="520px"
        style="border:none; border-radius:12px;"
        allowfullscreen></iframe>

*Click the LDR slider to change light level. Watch the LED respond in real time.*

## Build #2 — Arduino Code

```cpp
const int ldrPin = A0;
const int ledPin = 12;

int DARK_THRESHOLD = 600;  // 0–1023; tune this in class

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  int brightness = analogRead(ldrPin);      // 0 (bright) → 1023 (dark)
  if (brightness > DARK_THRESHOLD) {
    digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH);             // dark (high value) → LED ON
  } else {
    digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW);              // bright (low value) → LED OFF
  }
  delay(100);
}
```


## Exercise 13 — Q1: ADC Voltage {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

*(recall)* What voltage does `analogRead() = 512` represent?
*(Hint: it is half the full 5.0 V range)*

::: {.fragment}
**Answer:** 512 is half of 1023 ≈ half-scale → **2.5 V** (half of 5.0 V).
:::

## Exercise 13 — Q2: Change the Threshold {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

*(apply)* `DARK_THRESHOLD` is changed to **800**. Does the LED now switch on in dimmer or brighter conditions? Why?

::: {.fragment}
**Answer:** **Dimmer** — the sensor must return a *higher* value (800) before the LED switches on. Higher ADC values mean less light, so the room must be darker than before to trigger the LED.
:::

## Exercise 13 — Q3: Add a Second LED {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

*(apply)* Add a **second green LED** on Pin 11 that turns ON when the room is bright. Write the extra two lines of code.

::: {.fragment}
**Answer:**

```cpp
pinMode(11, OUTPUT);        // add in setup()

// inside loop(), add:
digitalWrite(11, brightness > DARK_THRESHOLD ? LOW : HIGH);
```

When bright, `brightness > DARK_THRESHOLD` is false → `LOW` is replaced by `HIGH`.
:::

## Exercise 13 — Q4: Stabilising Noisy Readings {background-color="#00C9A7" .sdaia-dark}

*(design)* The sensor reading can bounce slightly (noise). How could you use multiple readings or `delay()` to make the response more stable?

::: {.fragment}
**Answer:** Average several readings before deciding:

```cpp
long sum = 0;
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) sum += analogRead(A0);
int brightness = sum / 5;
```

Or only switch the LED if the condition holds for several consecutive loops — prevents flickering from brief spikes.
:::
witch the LED if the condition holds for two consecutive loops — prevents flickering from brief spikes.
:::
